request.question: Hail Flutie was a call made at the game with which managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network calling the action?

title: Norm Clarke
Jonathan Landman
A. Charles Muller
Hail Flutie
Martin Gottlieb
China Economic Information Network
Richard Stolley
Bill Adee
Doug Flutie
Brent Musburger
sentences: Norm Clarke (born 1942 in Terry, Montana) is an American gossip columnist in Las Vegas, Nevada.
He wrote the column "Vegas Confidential" for the "Las Vegas Review-Journal" from 1999 to 2016.
He currently writes the column "Vegas Diary" for the website of the Vegas Stats & Information Network.
His column covers celebrities and near-celebrities and their doings in the bright lights of "Sin City."
Jonathan I. Landman is an American journalist and deputy managing editor at "The New York Times".
Landman became deputy managing editor responsible for digital journalism for "The Times" in August 2005.
He had become assistant managing editor and member of "The Times" masthead in May 2005.
Prior to that, Landman had been acting culture editor from 2004 to 2005.
Previously, he was the assistant managing editor for enterprise from 2003 to 2004.
Landman had been metropolitan editor of "The Times" since 1999 and editor of "The Times"<nowiki>'</nowiki> Week in Review section since 1994.
Before that, he served as deputy editor of the Washington bureau since 1992; assistant editor in Washington from 1991; assistant metropolitan editor from 1990 and assistant national editor from 1989.
Landman joined "The Times" as a copy editor in 1987.
A. Charles Muller (born September 19, 1953) is an academic specializing in Korean Buddhism and East Asian Yogacara, having published numerous books and articles on these topics.
He is a resident of Japan, currently teaching at the University of Tokyo.

He is one of the earliest and most prolific developers of online research resources for the field of Buddhist Studies, being the founder and managing editor of the online Digital Dictionary of Buddhism, the CJKV-English Dictionary, and the H-Buddhism Scholars Information Network, along with having digitized and published numerous reference works.
The Hail Flutie game is a college football game that took place between the Boston College Eagles and the University of Miami Hurricanes on November 23, 1984.
It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports.
The game is most notable for a last-second Hail Mary pass from quarterback Doug Flutie to wide receiver Gerard Phelan to give Boston College the win.
Miami was the defending national champion and entered the game ranked 12th in the nation.
Boston College was ranked 10th with a record of 8–2 and had already accepted an invitation to the Cotton Bowl Classic at the end of the season.
The game was played at the Miami Orange Bowl, and televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating.
Martin Gottlieb is Assistant Managing Editor/Investigations of "Newsday".
From 2012 to 2016, he was Editor of "The Record" of Bergen County, New Jersey.
Previously, from 2008 to 2011, he was Global Editions Editor of the "New York Times"; in this capacity, he oversaw production of the "International Herald Tribune".
Gottlieb also served as Associate Managing Editor, Deputy Culture Editor and National Project Editor of the "Times".
Gottlieb was also Managing Editor of the "New York Daily News" and Editor-in-Chief of "The Village Voice".
China Economic Information Network (中国经济信息网) is a national information network that provides information and analyses of Chinese national macroeconomic trends.
It is sponsored by the State Information Network.
Richard Brockway Stolley (born October 3, 1928) is an American journalist and magazine editor.
He's most well known for his work at "Time" magazine, which he joined in 1953.
He subsequently held a number of roles at the magazine including report, writer, bureau chief, senior editor and managing editor.

In addition to his work at "Time", he has also been Assistant Managing Editor and Managing Editor of "Life" magazine, the founding Managing Editor of "People" magazine and Director of Special Projects for Time Inc
Bill Adee is Chief Operating Officer for Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN) after joining the network in March 2017.
Adee was formerly executive vice president of digital development and operations for the "Chicago Tribune".
He joined the news organization as its sports editor in the spring of 2002.

He previously worked for the "Chicago Sun-Times", "Los Angeles Daily News", "Akron Beacon Journal" of Akron, Ohio, "The Virginian-Pilot" of Norfolk, Virginia, "The Daily Herald' in Arlington Heights, Illinois and the "News Sun" of Waukegan, Illinois.
Douglas Richard Flutie (born October 23, 1962) is a former quarterback in the National Football League (NFL), Canadian Football League (CFL), and United States Football League (USFL).
He first rose to prominence during his college football career at Boston College, where he received the Heisman Trophy and the Davey O'Brien National Quarterback Award in 1984.
His "Hail Flutie" touchdown pass in a game against Miami on November 23, 1984 (dubbed "The Pass") is considered among the greatest moments in college football and American sports history.
Flutie was selected as the 285th pick in the 11th round of the 1985 NFL Draft by the Los Angeles Rams, making him the lowest drafted Heisman Award winner among those who were drafted.
Flutie played that year for the New Jersey Generals of the upstart USFL, having already signed a five-year $5 million contract with them prior to being drafted by the Rams.
In 1986, he signed with the NFL's Chicago Bears, and later played for the New England Patriots, becoming their starting quarterback in 1988.
Brent Woody Musburger ( ; born May 26, 1939) is an American sportscaster, lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN).
He retired in January 2017 from the ESPN and ABC television networks.

Formerly with CBS Sports and one of the original members of their program "The NFL Today", Musburger has also covered the NBA, MLB, NCAA football and basketball, and NASCAR and served as a studio host for games, a play-by-play man, and halftime host.
He has also performed postgame wrap-up segments and covered championship trophy presentations.
He is a member of the Montana Broadcaster's Association Hall of Fame.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: Hail Flutie was a call made at the game with which managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network calling the action?
hop1_docs: Vegas Stats & Information Network | VSiN is a national sports gambling news network based in Las Vegas, Nevada.  VSiN’s lead on-air personality is hall of fame broadcaster Brent Musburger, who anchors the weekday program called “My Guys in the Desert," which refers to Musburger’s veiled gambling references during his play-by-play of sports contests in earlier parts of his career.  Musburger left ESPN in January 2017 and joined VSiN.

Brent Musburger | Brent Woody Musburger ( ; born May 26, 1939) is an American sportscaster, lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN).  He retired in January 2017 from the ESPN and ABC television networks.  Formerly with CBS Sports and one of the original members of their program "The NFL Today", Musburger has also covered the NBA, MLB, NCAA football and basketball, and NASCAR and served as a studio host for games, a play-by-play man, and halftime host.  He has also performed postgame wrap-up segments and covered championship trophy presentations.  He is a member of the Montana Broadcaster's Association Hall of Fame.

Flutie effect | The Flutie effect or Flutie factor refers to the American phenomenon of having a successful college sports team increase the exposure and prominence of a university.  This is named after Boston College's Doug Flutie, whose successful Hail Mary pass in the 1984 game against the University of Miami clinched a win which allegedly played a large role in the increase in applications to Boston College the following year.

Hail Flutie | The Hail Flutie game is a college football game that took place between the Boston College Eagles and the University of Miami Hurricanes on November 23, 1984.  It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports.  The game is most notable for a last-second Hail Mary pass from quarterback Doug Flutie to wide receiver Gerard Phelan to give Boston College the win.  Miami was the defending national champion and entered the game ranked 12th in the nation.  Boston College was ranked 10th with a record of 8–2 and had already accepted an invitation to the Cotton Bowl Classic at the end of the season.  The game was played at the Miami Orange Bowl, and televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating.

Stat (system call) | stat() is a Unix system call that returns file attributes about an inode.  The semantics of stat() vary between operating systems.  As an example, Unix command ls uses this system call to retrieve information on files that includes:

Norm Clarke | Norm Clarke (born 1942 in Terry, Montana) is an American gossip columnist in Las Vegas, Nevada.  He wrote the column "Vegas Confidential" for the "Las Vegas Review-Journal" from 1999 to 2016.  He currently writes the column "Vegas Diary" for the website of the Vegas Stats & Information Network.  His column covers celebrities and near-celebrities and their doings in the bright lights of "Sin City."

Bill Adee | Bill Adee is Chief Operating Officer for Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN) after joining the network in March 2017.  Adee was formerly executive vice president of digital development and operations for the "Chicago Tribune".  He joined the news organization as its sports editor in the spring of 2002.  He previously worked for the "Chicago Sun-Times", "Los Angeles Daily News", "Akron Beacon Journal" of Akron, Ohio, "The Virginian-Pilot" of Norfolk, Virginia, "The Daily Herald' in Arlington Heights, Illinois and the "News Sun" of Waukegan, Illinois.
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Hail Flutie (1984 game) and Brent Musburger (VSiN managing editor).

- "November 23, 1984" — date of the Hail Flutie game (passage: game took place between Boston College and Miami).
- "televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating." (passage 4).
- Brent Musburger — described as "lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN)." (passage 2).
- Musburger "served as ... a play-by-play man" (passage 2).

Ambiguity / missing fact:
- Missing: an explicit statement that a specific commentator (which of the three named) actually made the on-air play-by-play "call" of the Hail Mary. Bridge entity: Brent Musburger (candidate because he is named on the telecast and has served as a play-by-play man).

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. Hail Flutie — open the article's "Telecast"/lede/"Broadcast" section to find which announcer made the play-by-play call (search page text for "call", "play-by-play", or "announc*").
2. Brent Musburger — inspect lede/infobox and "Career"/broadcasting section for confirmation linking him to calling the specific 1984 Miami–Boston College telecast or the Hail Mary moment.
3. Contemporary CBS broadcast credits or transcript (search: "CBS broadcast Hail Flutie 1984 Musburger call transcript" or video clip of the play) for definitive attribution.

It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports. VSiN’s lead on-air personality is hall of fame broadcaster Brent Musburger, who anchors the weekday program called “My Guys in the Desert," which refers to Musburger’s veiled gambling references during his play-by-play of sports contests in earlier parts of his career.  Musburger left ESPN in January 2017 and joined VSiN.

He retired in January 2017 from the ESPN and ABC television networks.  Formerly with CBS Sports and one of the original members of their program "The NFL Today", Musburger has also covered the NBA, MLB, NCAA football and basketball, and NASCAR and served as a studio host for games, a play-by-play man, and halftime host.  He has also performed postgame wrap-up segments and covered championship trophy presentations.  He is a member of the Montana Broadcaster's Association Hall of Fame.

Flutie effect | The Flutie effect or Flutie factor refers to the American phenomenon of having a successful college sports team increase the exposure and prominence of a university.  This is named after Boston College's Doug Flutie, whose successful Hail Mary pass in the 1984 game against the University of Miami clinched a win which allegedly played a large role in the increase in applications to Boston College the following year.

Doug Flutie | Douglas Richard Flutie (born October 23, 1962) is a former quarterback in the National Football League (NFL), Canadian Football League (CFL), and United States Football League (USFL).  He first rose to prominence during his college football career at Boston College, where he received the Heisman Trophy and the Davey O'Brien National Quarterback Award in 1984.  His "Hail Flutie" touchdown pass in a game against Miami on November 23, 1984 (dubbed "The Pass") is considered among the greatest moments in college football and American sports history.  Flutie was selected as the 285th pick in the 11th round of the 1985 NFL Draft by the Los Angeles Rams, making him the lowest drafted Heisman Award winner among those who were drafted.  Flutie played that year for the New Jersey Generals of the upstart USFL, having already signed a five-year $5 million contract with them prior to being drafted by the Rams.  In 1986, he signed with the NFL's Chicago Bears, and later played for the New England Patriots, becoming their starting quarterback in 1988.

1974 WTA Tour | The 1974 WTA Tour was composed of the fourth annual Virginia Slims Circuit and Women’s International Grand Prix, a tour of tennis tournaments for female tennis players, sponsored by Virginia Slims cigarettes.  The WTA signed their first television broadcast contract in 1974, with the broadcasting network CBS with Brent Musburger announcing.

Todd Musburger | Todd W. Musburger is a Chicago-based attorney specializing in media and entertainment law.  Since 1980 he has operated his own firm, Todd W. Musburger Ltd., which concentrates in representing individuals in the fields of television, radio, film, publishing and music.
response.summary_2: Brent Musburger, the managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network, called the action. Brent Musburger is VSiN’s lead broadcaster and managing editor and formerly served as a play-by-play announcer for CBS.
